svg.aria-label: Two coordination patterns side by side. On the left, HANDOFF: control and the full live trace are transferred from a generalist agent to a specialist. The generalist holds a 3-entry trace, identified order ORD-3300 for refund, looked up policy eligible, user confirmed they want the refund, then calls handoff to the billing-specialist carrying the trace and a clear role; the specialist refunds order ORD-3300 using the carried trace. Two failure modes are annotated. Truncation cuts the trace to the last entry so the order id is lost and the specialist cannot act. Role confusion carries the full trace but the brief never said who owns the task, so both agents stall. On the right, BLACKBOARD: many workers post their results to a shared append-only board, and the lead reads the whole board. Worker w1 posts the refund policy, w2 posts the warranty, w3 posts the shipping options, and the lead reads all three. The difference is that a handoff transfers control to one specialist while a blackboard shares results among many workers under one lead.
svg.title: Two coordination patterns: handoff transfers control plus the trace to a specialist; a blackboard shares results among workers under a lead

svg.text: w3 · Standard shipping is 3 to 5 business days; express...
entries are never overwritten; the lead reads the whole board
lead · reads the board, keeps control
no control transfer; the lead owns the task throughout and
synthesizes the posted results into one answer.
HANDOFF: control moves to ONE specialist; the trace is the
payload, so truncation or unclear ownership breaks it.
BLACKBOARD: results are SHARED among MANY workers; the
lead keeps control and composes the answer.

A handoff is a tool call that moves control plus the live trace to a specialist; a blackboard is shared memory many workers append to under one lead. Pick the handoff to delegate ownership, the blackboard to gather breadth.
